passages.explanation: The shift to Sibyl's world is jarring after the opulence of Lord Henry's Mayfair. The 'dingy sitting-room,' the single armchair, the 'shrill intrusive light' -- everything signals poverty and vulnerability. Mrs. Vane's immediate redirection from Sibyl's happiness to money and obligation reveals a woman who has learned to see love through the lens of economic survival. Sibyl's naive cry that 'love is more than money' is heartbreaking because the novel will prove the opposite: in Wilde's world, beauty and wealth protect, while poverty and sincerity destroy.

Mrs. Vane is one of Wilde's most complex minor characters: a failed actress who processes all of life through theatrical conventions. Her 'parrot-phrase' responses and 'crooked, false-jewelled fingers' suggest someone whose entire identity has become performance. Sibyl's declaration of love is described in terms that echo Dorian's own aesthetic language -- 'a rose shook in her blood' -- but where Dorian aestheticizes others, Sibyl aestheticizes herself. She is 'free in her prison of passion,' a paradox that captures how romantic love can feel like liberation while actually being a cage.

Mrs. Vane's shift from discouraging the romance to calculating whether the young man is rich enough for marriage exposes the mercenary reality beneath her theatrical sentiments. Sibyl's question about whether her mother loved her father 'as I love Prince Charming' opens a wound that connects to the novel's backstory: the Vane family's history, like Dorian's, is built on a romance that ended in ruin. Wilde is establishing a pattern where passionate love across class lines leads inevitably to tragedy.

James Vane's entrance introduces the novel's moral conscience in its roughest, most unpolished form. Where Lord Henry is all elegant surface, Jim is blunt physicality -- 'thick-set,' 'clumsy in movement,' hands and feet 'large.' Mrs. Vane's instinct to treat his arrival as a theatrical 'tableau' shows her pathological inability to experience genuine emotion without framing it as performance. The class contrast between Jim and Sibyl -- 'like a common gardener walking with a rose' -- foreshadows the violence that will erupt when the working-class world collides with Dorian's aristocratic sphere.

The conversation between Jim and his mother about Sibyl's mysterious suitor crackles with dramatic irony. Mrs. Vane's eager speculation that the man 'is probably a member of the aristocracy' reveals her willing blindness: she would rather her daughter be a rich man's mistress than ask uncomfortable questions. Jim's blunt suspicion cuts through all her theatrical evasions. The scene exposes how class aspiration and economic desperation conspire to endanger young women, with mothers as complicit as seducers.

Mrs. Vane's affectations reach their peak as she 'looks up to the ceiling in search of an imaginary gallery' while saying goodbye, literally performing motherhood for an audience that does not exist. Jim's hatred of these affectations reflects a deeper disgust with the inauthenticity that pervades every level of society in the novel. Wilde suggests that the aestheticism of the upper classes has its degraded mirror in the theatricality of the lower, and both are forms of self-deception.

Sibyl's fantasy of Jim's Australian adventures -- gold nuggets, rescued heiresses, mounted policemen -- is touching in its naivety but also reveals how deeply the language of melodrama and romance has shaped her understanding of reality. She can only imagine life through fictional templates, which is precisely the quality that makes her a great actress and a terrible judge of actual human beings. Jim's brooding awareness of 'the danger of Sibyl's position' and the maxim that 'children begin by loving their parents; as they grow older they judge them' introduce a hard, clear-eyed moral perspective that is entirely absent from the glittering world of Lord Henry and Dorian.

The siblings' argument about Prince Charming is a collision between romantic idealism and protective realism. Sibyl's responses are all theatrical -- 'to see him is to worship him; to know him is to trust him' -- while Jim's are all instinct: 'He wants to enslave you.' Neither can reach the other because they are speaking different languages entirely. Sibyl's insistence that 'love makes people good' is the novel's most important claim and will be tested ruthlessly in the chapters ahead. Jim's vow to kill anyone who hurts his sister is dismissed as melodrama, but Wilde plants it as a genuine threat that will resurface.

The glimpse of Dorian driving past in an open carriage -- golden-haired, laughing, flanked by ladies -- presents him as a creature from another world entirely, one of wealth and ease that the Vanes can only observe from a park bench. Jim's demand to see this 'Prince Charming' and his oath to kill him 'as sure as there is a God in heaven' is both absurd melodrama and deadly serious foreshadowing. The class gulf between the watchers and the watched is the novel's unspoken engine of tragedy.

Sibyl's dismissal of Jim's anger as 'silly melodrama' is deeply ironic given that their mother actually performed in melodramas and their entire family processes reality through theatrical frameworks. The siblings' parting -- his 'fierce murderous hatred' softened by her arms around his neck -- captures the impossible tension between love and rage that Jim embodies. His jealousy is not just fraternal but existential: Dorian represents everything Jim is not, and the class system that separates them ensures they can never meet as equals.

The chapter's devastating final sequence reveals the Vane family's deepest secret: Jim is illegitimate, the son of a gentleman who was 'not free' to marry his mother. This revelation reframes everything -- Mrs. Vane's theatrical evasiveness, her fixation on whether Sibyl's suitor is 'highly connected,' Jim's instinctive class hatred. The scene's bitter masterpiece is its ending: Mrs. Vane, told by her son that he will 'kill like a dog' anyone who wrongs Sibyl, admires the threat as good theatre. She 'would have liked to have continued the scene on the same emotional scale.' Even genuine danger is processed as performance, and she consoles herself by rehearsing her son's departing phrases. Wilde shows us a woman so thoroughly consumed by artifice that she has lost access to authentic feeling, making her the novel's darkest commentary on the relationship between art and life.
